---
title: "Government Documents Chatbot Project"
---

# Storyboarding

## ChatGPT AI Product Storyboard Prompt

You are advising a creator who is seeking venture capital funding for an early-stage civic technology project.

The creator has the vision, but not the technical programming skills required to build the system. Attached are several PNG files showing illustration examples used to teach YouTube viewers the technical concepts handled by different programming specialists involved in deploying a large-scale natural-language chatbot. I love this loose, whiteboard-sketch style. Do not copy these images directly — they are the work of another creator. Instead, emulate the style and formatting only, and do not include the original creator’s likeness or characters.  Where the uploaded illustrations are based on for-profit entities, use labels and examples of government-style situations (Mangers = Citizens or maybe Researchers, Sales = Revenues, etc.)

The new illustrations should show the key questions, decisions, and technical challenges involved in building a useful and successful chatbot. They should also highlight the kinds of expertise needed to solve them. A set of 10 to 12 landscape-format illustrations should be sufficient.

The creator needs a storyboard-style investor presentation that clearly explains the project’s conceptual framework, the market need, and the case for first-round funding. The purpose of the presentation is not to provide detailed financial projections, since no reliable financial data yet exist. Instead, it should help investors understand the problem, the opportunity, and why first-round funding is needed to research, design, and validate the right technical architecture.


First-round funding would be used to:


Purchase or hire the expertise needed to identify an appropriate and scalable system architecture.


Evaluate the feasibility of building an enterprise-grade municipal transparency platform.


Develop a realistic feasibility budget.


Determine the technical, operational, and financial requirements for moving beyond prototype stage.



Core Problem

Municipal governments often post public documents on their websites in the name of transparency. These documents may technically be available for download, but in practice they are extremely difficult for ordinary citizens, council members, journalists, and even staff to use for meaningful analysis.
Most of the information is buried in PDFs, agenda packets, minutes, budget documents, spreadsheets, and other formats that are often unstructured, inconsistent, and difficult to search.
As a result, a system that is intended to make government “transparent” often has the practical effect of keeping important information opaque.
Citizens may be able to download documents, but they usually cannot easily answer questions such as:


What has the city spent on this issue over time?


When was this policy first discussed?


How have staff recommendations changed across years?


What documents support this decision?


What historical context should council members consider before voting?


The information exists, but it is scattered, siloed, and difficult to analyze.

Why the Current System Fails
Municipal records are usually organized as individual documents rather than as connected knowledge.
This creates several barriers:


Information is trapped in PDFs and other difficult formats.
Agenda packets, minutes, reports, budgets, and attachments are often posted as static files.


Documents are siloed.
A citizen may need to search across years of agendas, packets, minutes, budgets, and financial reports to understand one issue.


Keyword search is limited.
Basic search can find exact words or phrases, but it does not understand meaning, context, synonyms, or related concepts.


Historical analysis is extremely difficult.
Multi-year comparisons require extraordinary manual effort or data-processing skills.


The burden is shifted to the citizen.
In practice, the citizen must have data-science, records-management, and policy-analysis skills to extract useful insight.


The result is a transparency gap: documents are public, but the knowledge inside them is not practically accessible.

Existing Prototype
A simple Shiny app prototype has already been created using a keyword-matching approach.
This prototype was built around the question:

“Where does this word appear?”

The system maps keywords and phrases to municipal documents. When a user searches for a specific word or phrase, the app returns matching documents where those words appear.
This is useful and powerful as a first step. Keyword matching still powers much of internet search today.
However, it has a fundamental limitation:

Keyword search treats words as symbols, not meaning.

It does not understand synonyms, ambiguity, context, relationships among documents, or more complex user intent.
For example, a keyword search may find the word “drainage,” but it may not understand that a user is really asking about stormwater infrastructure, capital improvement planning, recurring neighborhood complaints, or long-term budget commitments.

Proposed Solution
The next step is a natural-language municipal transparency chatbot.
The proposed system would allow users to ask plain-English questions and receive source-backed answers drawn from municipal records.
Instead of asking:

“Where does this word appear?”

The user could ask:

“What has the city done about drainage complaints in this neighborhood over the past five years?”

Or:

“How has the police department’s compensation budget changed since 2020?”

Or:

“What documents should I read before understanding this council vote?”

The chatbot should be able to:


Search across municipal documents.


Identify relevant records even when exact keywords differ.


Link related concepts across years and document types.


Provide clear citations to source documents.


Summarize historical context.


Support more analytical questions from citizens, council members, staff, journalists, and civic organizations.


The system should function more like ChatGPT or Claude, but with answers grounded in a municipality’s own records.

Marketplace Need
Municipal transparency is a widespread problem.
Cities, counties, school districts, and other public entities routinely publish large volumes of documents, but those documents are rarely organized in a way that supports meaningful public understanding.
The practical effect is that:


Citizens cannot easily evaluate the quality of decisions.


Council members lack quick access to historical context.


Staff spend time responding to records requests that might be reduced through better self-service access.


Journalists and civic groups must perform time-consuming manual searches.


Public trust suffers because the information is technically available but practically inaccessible.


A municipal transparency chatbot could serve multiple audiences:


Citizens who want plain-language answers about public decisions.


Council members who need historical context before voting.


City staff who need faster access to prior actions, reports, and policies.


Journalists who need to trace issues across time.


Civic organizations that monitor public spending and governance.


Municipal governments that want to reduce repetitive FOIA or public-information requests.



Value Proposition
The proposed platform would turn municipal document archives into a searchable, source-backed civic knowledge system.
Its value would come from making public information:


easier to find,


easier to understand,


easier to verify,


easier to analyze,


and easier to connect across time.


The system would not replace official records. Instead, it would help users find, interpret, and cite those records more effectively.
The core promise is:

Public documents should not merely be posted. They should be usable.


Funding Objective
The purpose of first-round funding is to move from a working keyword-search prototype toward a technically validated, scalable, natural-language municipal records assistant.
Funding would support research and expert consultation to determine:


The optimal system architecture.


The appropriate document-processing pipeline.


The right approach to chunking, embeddings, retrieval, and citations.


The feasibility of enterprise-scale deployment.


Security, privacy, and governance requirements.


Cost estimates for development, hosting, maintenance, and scaling.


A realistic product roadmap from prototype to pilot.


The presentation should persuade investors that this is a serious civic technology opportunity with a clear public-sector need, a working early prototype, and a logical path toward a scalable product.

Desired Output
Create a storyboard-style investor presentation that explains:


The problem with current municipal transparency.


Why posting documents online is not enough.


How keyword search helps but falls short.


Why natural-language AI creates a new opportunity.


Who would benefit from the system.


How the proposed chatbot would work conceptually.


Why first-round funding is needed.


What questions the funding would help answer.


What a successful next-stage prototype or pilot could demonstrate.


The tone should be clear, persuasive, and investor-oriented, while still grounded in the civic purpose of improving public access to government information.

Avoid unsupported financial projections. Emphasize the market problem, user pain points, existing prototype, technical opportunity, and need for feasibility funding.




## Gemini AI Product Storyboard Prompt

Abject failure!!!




## Claude AI Product Storyboard Prompt

You are advising a creator who is seeking venture capital funding for an early-stage civic technology project.

The creator has the vision, but not the technical programming skills required to build the system. Attached are several PNG files showing illustration examples used to teach YouTube viewers the technical concepts handled by different programming specialists involved in deploying a large-scale natural-language chatbot. I love this loose, whiteboard-sketch style. Do not copy these images directly — they are the work of another creator. Instead, emulate the style and formatting only, and do not include the original creator’s likeness or characters.  Where the uploaded illustrations are based on for-profit entities, use labels and examples of government-style situations (Mangers = Citizens or maybe Researchers, Sales = Revenues, etc.)

The new illustrations should show the key questions, decisions, and technical challenges involved in building a useful and successful chatbot. They should also highlight the kinds of expertise needed to solve them. A set of 10 to 12 landscape-format illustrations should be sufficient.

The creator needs a storyboard-style investor presentation that clearly explains the project’s conceptual framework, the market need, and the case for first-round funding. The purpose of the presentation is not to provide detailed financial projections, since no reliable financial data yet exist. Instead, it should help investors understand the problem, the opportunity, and why first-round funding is needed to research, design, and validate the right technical architecture.


First-round funding would be used to:


Purchase or hire the expertise needed to identify an appropriate and scalable system architecture.


Evaluate the feasibility of building an enterprise-grade municipal transparency platform.


Develop a realistic feasibility budget.


Determine the technical, operational, and financial requirements for moving beyond prototype stage.



Core Problem

Municipal governments often post public documents on their websites in the name of transparency. These documents may technically be available for download, but in practice they are extremely difficult for ordinary citizens, council members, journalists, and even staff to use for meaningful analysis.
Most of the information is buried in PDFs, agenda packets, minutes, budget documents, spreadsheets, and other formats that are often unstructured, inconsistent, and difficult to search.
As a result, a system that is intended to make government “transparent” often has the practical effect of keeping important information opaque.
Citizens may be able to download documents, but they usually cannot easily answer questions such as:


What has the city spent on this issue over time?


When was this policy first discussed?


How have staff recommendations changed across years?


What documents support this decision?


What historical context should council members consider before voting?


The information exists, but it is scattered, siloed, and difficult to analyze.

Why the Current System Fails
Municipal records are usually organized as individual documents rather than as connected knowledge.
This creates several barriers:


Information is trapped in PDFs and other difficult formats.
Agenda packets, minutes, reports, budgets, and attachments are often posted as static files.


Documents are siloed.
A citizen may need to search across years of agendas, packets, minutes, budgets, and financial reports to understand one issue.


Keyword search is limited.
Basic search can find exact words or phrases, but it does not understand meaning, context, synonyms, or related concepts.


Historical analysis is extremely difficult.
Multi-year comparisons require extraordinary manual effort or data-processing skills.


The burden is shifted to the citizen.
In practice, the citizen must have data-science, records-management, and policy-analysis skills to extract useful insight.


The result is a transparency gap: documents are public, but the knowledge inside them is not practically accessible.

Existing Prototype
A simple Shiny app prototype has already been created using a keyword-matching approach.
This prototype was built around the question:

“Where does this word appear?”

The system maps keywords and phrases to municipal documents. When a user searches for a specific word or phrase, the app returns matching documents where those words appear.
This is useful and powerful as a first step. Keyword matching still powers much of internet search today.
However, it has a fundamental limitation:

Keyword search treats words as symbols, not meaning.

It does not understand synonyms, ambiguity, context, relationships among documents, or more complex user intent.
For example, a keyword search may find the word “drainage,” but it may not understand that a user is really asking about stormwater infrastructure, capital improvement planning, recurring neighborhood complaints, or long-term budget commitments.

Proposed Solution
The next step is a natural-language municipal transparency chatbot.
The proposed system would allow users to ask plain-English questions and receive source-backed answers drawn from municipal records.
Instead of asking:

“Where does this word appear?”

The user could ask:

“What has the city done about drainage complaints in this neighborhood over the past five years?”

Or:

“How has the police department’s compensation budget changed since 2020?”

Or:

“What documents should I read before understanding this council vote?”

The chatbot should be able to:


Search across municipal documents.


Identify relevant records even when exact keywords differ.


Link related concepts across years and document types.


Provide clear citations to source documents.


Summarize historical context.


Support more analytical questions from citizens, council members, staff, journalists, and civic organizations.


The system should function more like ChatGPT or Claude, but with answers grounded in a municipality’s own records.

Marketplace Need
Municipal transparency is a widespread problem.
Cities, counties, school districts, and other public entities routinely publish large volumes of documents, but those documents are rarely organized in a way that supports meaningful public understanding.
The practical effect is that:


Citizens cannot easily evaluate the quality of decisions.


Council members lack quick access to historical context.


Staff spend time responding to records requests that might be reduced through better self-service access.


Journalists and civic groups must perform time-consuming manual searches.


Public trust suffers because the information is technically available but practically inaccessible.


A municipal transparency chatbot could serve multiple audiences:


Citizens who want plain-language answers about public decisions.


Council members who need historical context before voting.


City staff who need faster access to prior actions, reports, and policies.


Journalists who need to trace issues across time.


Civic organizations that monitor public spending and governance.


Municipal governments that want to reduce repetitive FOIA or public-information requests.



Value Proposition
The proposed platform would turn municipal document archives into a searchable, source-backed civic knowledge system.
Its value would come from making public information:


easier to find,


easier to understand,


easier to verify,


easier to analyze,


and easier to connect across time.


The system would not replace official records. Instead, it would help users find, interpret, and cite those records more effectively.
The core promise is:

Public documents should not merely be posted. They should be usable.


Funding Objective
The purpose of first-round funding is to move from a working keyword-search prototype toward a technically validated, scalable, natural-language municipal records assistant.
Funding would support research and expert consultation to determine:


The optimal system architecture.


The appropriate document-processing pipeline.


The right approach to chunking, embeddings, retrieval, and citations.


The feasibility of enterprise-scale deployment.


Security, privacy, and governance requirements.


Cost estimates for development, hosting, maintenance, and scaling.


A realistic product roadmap from prototype to pilot.


The presentation should persuade investors that this is a serious civic technology opportunity with a clear public-sector need, a working early prototype, and a logical path toward a scalable product.

Desired Output
Create a storyboard-style investor presentation that explains:


The problem with current municipal transparency.


Why posting documents online is not enough.


How keyword search helps but falls short.


Why natural-language AI creates a new opportunity.


Who would benefit from the system.


How the proposed chatbot would work conceptually.


Why first-round funding is needed.


What questions the funding would help answer.


What a successful next-stage prototype or pilot could demonstrate.


The tone should be clear, persuasive, and investor-oriented, while still grounded in the civic purpose of improving public access to government information.

Avoid unsupported financial projections. Emphasize the market problem, user pain points, existing prototype, technical opportunity, and need for feasibility funding.




## ChatGPT AI Technology Challenges Storyboard Prompt

You are advising a creator who is seeking venture capital funding for an early-stage civic technology project.

The creator has the vision, but not the technical programming skills required to build the system. Attached are several PNG files showing illustration examples used to teach YouTube viewers the technical concepts handled by different programming specialists involved in deploying a large-scale natural-language chatbot. I love this loose, whiteboard-sketch style. Do not copy these images directly — they are the work of another creator. Instead, emulate the style and formatting only and you can freely use the technology content to suit our presentation, and do not include the original creator’s likeness or characters.  Where the uploaded illustrations are based on for-profit entities, use labels and examples of government-style situations (Mangers = Citizens or maybe Researchers, Sales = Revenues, etc.)

The new illustrations should show the key technology challenges in the order they must be addressed involved in building a useful and successful chatbot. They should also highlight the kinds of expertise needed to solve them. A set of 10 to 12 landscape-format illustrations should be sufficient.

The creator needs a storyboard-style investor 'technology primer' presentation that clearly explains the project’s conceptual framework and the technology decisions to be made.  The viewers are NOT technology savvy, but must have an appreciation for the system to be built.  The purpose of the presentation is not to provide detailed financial projections, since no reliable financial data yet exist. Instead, it should help investors understand the technology stack that is required, and the type of expert consultants required.

From the project details below (provided for context), use only the elements that reflect the technology available and the decisions that must be made.


First-round funding would be used to:


Purchase or hire the expertise needed to identify an appropriate and scalable system architecture.


Evaluate the feasibility of building an enterprise-grade municipal transparency platform.


Develop a realistic feasibility budget.


Determine the technical, operational, and financial requirements for moving beyond prototype stage.



Core Problem

Municipal governments often post public documents on their websites in the name of transparency. These documents may technically be available for download, but in practice they are extremely difficult for ordinary citizens, council members, journalists, and even staff to use for meaningful analysis.
Most of the information is buried in PDFs, agenda packets, minutes, budget documents, spreadsheets, and other formats that are often unstructured, inconsistent, and difficult to search.
As a result, a system that is intended to make government “transparent” often has the practical effect of keeping important information opaque.
Citizens may be able to download documents, but they usually cannot easily answer questions such as:


What has the city spent on this issue over time?


When was this policy first discussed?


How have staff recommendations changed across years?


What documents support this decision?


What historical context should council members consider before voting?


The information exists, but it is scattered, siloed, and difficult to analyze.

Why the Current System Fails
Municipal records are usually organized as individual documents rather than as connected knowledge.
This creates several barriers:


Information is trapped in PDFs and other difficult formats.
Agenda packets, minutes, reports, budgets, and attachments are often posted as static files.


Documents are siloed.
A citizen may need to search across years of agendas, packets, minutes, budgets, and financial reports to understand one issue.


Keyword search is limited.
Basic search can find exact words or phrases, but it does not understand meaning, context, synonyms, or related concepts.


Historical analysis is extremely difficult.
Multi-year comparisons require extraordinary manual effort or data-processing skills.


The burden is shifted to the citizen.
In practice, the citizen must have data-science, records-management, and policy-analysis skills to extract useful insight.


The result is a transparency gap: documents are public, but the knowledge inside them is not practically accessible.

Existing Prototype
A simple Shiny app prototype has already been created using a keyword-matching approach.
This prototype was built around the question:

“Where does this word appear?”

The system maps keywords and phrases to municipal documents. When a user searches for a specific word or phrase, the app returns matching documents where those words appear.
This is useful and powerful as a first step. Keyword matching still powers much of internet search today.
However, it has a fundamental limitation:

Keyword search treats words as symbols, not meaning.

It does not understand synonyms, ambiguity, context, relationships among documents, or more complex user intent.
For example, a keyword search may find the word “drainage,” but it may not understand that a user is really asking about stormwater infrastructure, capital improvement planning, recurring neighborhood complaints, or long-term budget commitments.

Proposed Solution

The next step is a natural-language municipal transparency chatbot.
The proposed system would allow users to ask plain-English questions and receive source-backed answers drawn from municipal records.
Instead of asking:

“Where does this word appear?”

The user could ask:

“What has the city done about drainage complaints in this neighborhood over the past five years?”

Or:

“How has the police department’s compensation budget changed since 2020?”

Or:

“What documents should I read before understanding this council vote?”

The chatbot should be able to:


Search across municipal documents.


Identify relevant records even when exact keywords differ.


Link related concepts across years and document types.


Provide clear citations to source documents.


Summarize historical context.


Support more analytical questions from citizens, council members, staff, journalists, and civic organizations.


The system should function more like ChatGPT or Claude, but with answers grounded in a municipality’s own records.

Marketplace Need

Municipal transparency is a widespread problem.
Cities, counties, school districts, and other public entities routinely publish large volumes of documents, but those documents are rarely organized in a way that supports meaningful public understanding.
The practical effect is that:


Citizens cannot easily evaluate the quality of decisions.


Council members lack quick access to historical context.


Staff spend time responding to records requests that might be reduced through better self-service access.


Journalists and civic groups must perform time-consuming manual searches.


Public trust suffers because the information is technically available but practically inaccessible.


A municipal transparency chatbot could serve multiple audiences:


Citizens who want plain-language answers about public decisions.


Council members who need historical context before voting.


City staff who need faster access to prior actions, reports, and policies.


Journalists who need to trace issues across time.


Civic organizations that monitor public spending and governance.


Municipal governments that want to reduce repetitive FOIA or public-information requests.



Value Proposition

The proposed platform would turn municipal document archives into a searchable, source-backed civic knowledge system.
Its value would come from making public information:


easier to find,


easier to understand,


easier to verify,


easier to analyze,


and easier to connect across time.


The system would not replace official records. Instead, it would help users find, interpret, and cite those records more effectively.
The core promise is:

Public documents should not merely be posted. They should be usable.


Funding Objective

The purpose of first-round funding is to move from a working keyword-search prototype toward a technically validated, scalable, natural-language municipal records assistant.
Funding would support research and expert consultation to determine:


The optimal system architecture.


The appropriate document-processing pipeline.


The right approach to chunking, embeddings, retrieval, and citations.


The feasibility of enterprise-scale deployment.


Security, privacy, and governance requirements.


Cost estimates for development, hosting, maintenance, and scaling.


A realistic product roadmap from prototype to pilot.


The presentation should persuade investors that this is a serious civic technology opportunity with a clear public-sector need, a working early prototype, and a logical path toward a scalable product.

Desired Output

Create a storyboard-style investor presentation that explains:


The problem with current municipal transparency.


Why posting documents online is not enough.


How keyword search helps but falls short.


Why natural-language AI creates a new opportunity.


Who would benefit from the system.


How the proposed chatbot would work conceptually.


Why first-round funding is needed.


What questions the funding would help answer.


What a successful next-stage prototype or pilot could demonstrate.


The tone should be clear, persuasive, and investor-oriented, while still grounded in the civic purpose of improving public access to government information.

Avoid unsupported financial projections. Emphasize the market problem, user pain points, existing prototype, technical opportunity, and need for feasibility funding.
